item.content:encoded: Yay, we did it!!! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't the foundation be using kilograms instead of pounds?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Party time! 🎂, although that's some time ago, It is still a celebration!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the Foundation having a process to decommission SCPs. In particular I love that the anomaly they're getting rid of isn't some monster that will kill everyone but rather something that is bankrupting the Foundation and risking their international relations (I'm sure other world governments would be unhappy to learn about the Foundation sending huge amounts of money to past-Spain and would pressure for decommissioning as well).

I also buy your idea about the difference between decommissioned and neutralized. +1

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Woo, congrats! 🎉
Oh yeah, we hit +100. Forgot about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is short and sweet, and everybody seems to be throwing a hissy fit about the decommissioned classification, though. Regardless, +1, good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 Good stuff. I hope more in-universe decommissioning are written. They can't contain Keter-classes forever, especially if neutralization is easier to enact than containment.

But I got to ask something. What's the Difference between the Anomalous Chronology Department and the Temporal Anomalies Department? Do they study and catalogue CK-Class Restructuring events, hypothesize anomalous history before the Foundation started, and if their security clearance is high enough, catalogue every Ennui Protocol or SCP-2000 activation enacted due to an anomalous event?

item.title: why
item.content:encoded: No one expects the Spanish Decommission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A solid article although it's pretty obvious its purpose is to show a case where decommissioning is necessary.

I understand why many might be upset by the precedent this sets- aka articles in the future destroying stuff "because we can" - but it's very clearly shown that this takes place only in exceptional circumstances.

The foundation at this point holds about 5000 scps and although its resources are vast, they aren't infinite (as shown by many other articles). Decommissioning feels like something any real life organisation with these expenses and responsibilities would be forced to do.

+1 for bringing the concept back with a very good example of when it would be necessary

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Yes, I like this.

I haven't seen the Decommissioned object class used in….well it's been a long time since it was something that wasn't an obvious joke (I absolutely hate lolFoundation and go out of my way to avoid reading those articles when possible), maybe literally never? I like this use of it, and I like that it formalizes a procedure that future writers can use. Hopefully it never becomes a cliche, but, yeah this is an easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For your stuff about the Decommissioned class, I answered a similar question here.

As for the ACS stuff, that's more of a personal stylistic choice that I enjoy using; if you don't like it, that's alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why have both standard and ACS classifications? The only reason I can see is to try and please everyone, but that misses the point of design elements - both standard and ACS classifications should be striving for simplicity and stripping unnecessary information from the point of view of being aesthetically pleasing as well as a smoother narrative experience, so having both is strictly worse.

I don't think decommissioned as a class adds anything beyond what is already on offer with neutralized. The class reflects the current state of the object (re: containment) - if you introduce 'how did this object get into this state' as part of the criteria this is fluff none of the other classes have. The decom form also looks like more of a meme template rather than something a real organisation would actually use - for an action which is presumably rarely done and only in extreme circumstances, pro-forma checkboxes with generic and poorly-detailed answers are not fit for purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The initial description is clumsy and uninteresting. I can't really picture how this was stored, and the lack of description of the attacks was a wasted opportunity. I don't really see what the letter adds (e.g., it's a story thread that's just thrown out and never resolved), whereas more description of the attacks throughout history would've added some depth and mental images.

Including the dollar amount seems silly. Precious metals change in value all the time, and "2,500'<unit>s of silver, or gold and gemstomes equivalent in value" or something like that is a much meatier image. If you want to include the dollar amount, I would put that down in the section where they discover the financial link.

The reuse of decommissioning was… fine. The form was an interesting addition. I don't like the footnote: marking it as a change in policy rather than just a truth in this article's canon feels like an attempt to explain irl site culture in-universe, and I don't really like the stories that leads to (especially if the bar seems low, which having a short form, even if neat, implies). If you just wanted to include a narrative thread about this department being founded, this was a really uninteresting way to do that.

The best ideas in the article were around the temporal/monetary shenanigans. Those were quite good.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may point out that the "original charm" of the Decommissioned class was to shame bad writers. You're basically advocating for another lolFoundation SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and has nothing to do with the original intention of the object class by the old guard in the first place

See, it's in the name only. No shitty skip, no colorful termination, nothing. Just a typical modern remake of classic work without any of its charm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty sure that mention -D in regular article is mostly avoid. Isn't the censored skip in Infinite Pizza Box was actually Duke?

I've never seen anyone consider it taboo to mention a -D in an article. In fact, I did so myself, in 4745.

I'm all with bring back -D, but this one is -D in the name only.

More than just in name, since the object was indeed Decommissioned.

its terminate log

Hate to break it to you, but that wasn't a termination log. That was a proposal for bringing back the Decommissioned class. The termination was summed up as follows:

The Decommissioning consisted of SCP-4456-D being incinerated completely; any remains in the form of ash or char spontaneously disappeared.

Finally, I think you seem to be confused as to what aspects of Decommissioning I'm bringing back here. My focus is on the meaning of the Decommissioned class in-universe, and has nothing to do with the original intention of the object class by the old guard in the first place. If it helps, think of the Decommissioned class as another esoteric class: here, it refers to objects that were officially destroyed by the Foundation. That's the definition of the Decommissioned class, according to myself, and inferred from all the other Decommissioned SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To counterpoint, the object was very clearly destroyed, judging from Dante's letter. And since there have been no more appearances of the object, I would say the Decommissioning was successful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but it still serves a purpose for the Foundation to include it.

I'm pretty sure that mention -D in regular article is mostly avoid. Isn't the censored skip in Infinite Pizza Box was actually Duke?

Decommissioned is the Foundation actively trying to Neutralize (usually by killing or destroying) the object itself.

In entertaining (to reader) but disgraceful manner (to the skip…and possibly author), if I might add. I'm all with bring back -D, but this one is -D in the name only. In fact, the skip itself is in +20, safe zone to stay on wiki, but its terminate log was -10. That's just not it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Decommissioned is a piece of shit that would be just deleted in these days and the object would be (technically speaking) removed from database like it never happen.

Out-of-universe, yes. It's out of practice, but it still serves a purpose for the Foundation to include it.

I'll revoke my vote if it's changed to Neutralized, which is something destroyed or nullified in-universe.

Neutralized usually means that the anomaly itself has been destroyed or has had their anomalous properties taken away from them. Decommissioned is the Foundation actively trying to Neutralize (usually by killing or destroying) the object itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ignore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fill-out form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I said that I like the concept of Decommissioning in the chat, but this is one of the worse case for Decommissioning. The item clearly capable of changing causality at will, as well as manifesting copies of itself, which means there's no real guarantee that the item is really destroyed and not just a copy while it escaped through time. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't make sense for this to demand $500,000 in bullion - as a 15th century Spanish Galleon, it would have no proper conception of 21st century American dollars. It should either demand an amount in Spanish dollars (pieces of eight) or a weight. Given that the pieces of eight exchange rate is going to incredibly hard to calculate, weight makes the most sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote. Decommissioned is a piece of shit that would be just deleted in these days and the object would be (technically speaking) removed from database like it never happen. They're often on So Bad It's Good shitty skip, and you fail at that.

I'll revoke my vote if it's changed to Neutralized, which is something destroyed or nullified in-universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your feedback! Just curious, which report are you talking about? The fill-out form or the separate page?

Also, thanks for pointing out the typo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The actual anomaly is a bit on the boring side but I really like that you brought back Decommissioned Class. I also like the report that specified the ground rules for when the Foundation would decommision a skip, although I do think some of it could be otherwise omitted from the final product.

Eta: you misspelled "Appolyon".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First and foremost, thanks to all the people who went over this! Ihp, IronDruid, and Dyslexion, you guys were a huge help. And a very special thanks to gee0765 for making the ACS header for the Decommissioned class, as well as a big thanks to Calibri Bold for designing the Decommissioned icon. To all the rest of you who helped in any way that I have forgotten about, please make yourselves known, or even PM me so that I can put you in the credits list.

Image found here.

My reasons for making this.
I hope this provides some clarity.

First, I wanted to use the Decommissioned class, but without any lolFoundation elements. I noticed that Decom. objects could be considered "canon," and so I wondered what the difference between Decommissioned and Neutralized was in-universe. After some research and talking around, I concluded that Decommissioned objects were intentionally destroyed by the Foundation, while Neutralized ones were objects that were destroyed or deanomalized for any other reason.

Second, I noticed that almost all objects that were destroyed by the Foundation (save for previous Decom. items) were these massive, world-ending forces. I figured that there could conceivably be other reasons to destroy an object, though it would still be done very rarely. What about expense? Would the Foundation spend millions or even billions of dollars to contain an object, even if it had no use to them, and it could be destroyed fairly easily? Or how about legality? The Foundation is still subject to various laws imposed by the governments and entities supporting its existence, so what if the only way to contain an anomaly was in violation of those laws?
